ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tandoori prawns or saffron-marinated grilled prawns. FOODON_03302051 (shrimp food product) is used as the closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Shrimps and prawns) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for tandoori-style marinated grilled prawns.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 (Prawn - food) is the closest parent. No specific concept exists for tandoori or saffron-marinated preparations.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Zafrani Tandoori Prawns is approximately 250 g, comprising 6 to 8 large prawns with marinade residue and accompanied by mint chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Prawn (grilled, marinated in yogurt and spices) — estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and USDA FoodData Central (Shrimp, cooked; Yogurt, plain, whole milk)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Zafrani Tandoori Prawns exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for cooked prawns (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2017 edition) with USDA FoodData Central data for whole-milk yogurt and cream marinade components. Saffron contributes negligible macronutrients at typical usage levels. The composite reflects approximately 70% prawn flesh post-cooking and 30% marinade contribution. Values are consistent with published ranges for tandoori seafood preparations.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein-dominant seafood and mixed Indian dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked shrimp/prawn and yogurt-based marinade; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Zafrani Tandoori Prawns. GI is estimated at 15 based on the dominant protein-fat composition of the dish (cooked prawns ~70%, yogurt-cream marinade ~30%). Cooked shrimp and yogurt are both low-GI foods (GI < 20 and GI ~36 respectively per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008). The low carbohydrate content (3.8 g/100g) and high protein content (18.5 g/100g) strongly support a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (15 × 9.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.4, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade spices and salt
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g serving provides approximately 1050 mg sodium, representing about 46% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation when possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 15 and only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Zafrani Tandoori Prawns have a negligible glycemic load and are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content and saturated fat from cream-yogurt marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns are naturally high in dietary cholesterol (~148 mg/100g) and the cream-based marinade contributes saturated fat (2.9 g/100g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and consider requesting a reduced-cream marinade, while benefiting from the high-quality lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids present in the prawns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Zafrani Tandoori Prawns is an Indian restaurant dish featuring large prawns marinated in a creamy, spiced mixture of yogurt, fresh cream, saffron (zafran), ginger, garlic, red chilli, garam masala, and lemon juice, then cooked at high temperature in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The saffron imparts a distinctive golden-orange hue and a subtle floral-earthy aroma that distinguishes this preparation from standard tandoori prawns. The tandoor cooking method produces a lightly charred exterior with a juicy, tender interior, delivering a complex flavour profile that is simultaneously smoky, spiced, and mildly creamy. The dish is typically served with fresh mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. Nutritionally, Zafrani Tandoori Prawns are high in lean protein (approximately 18.5 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (6.8 g per 100 g, primarily from the dairy marinade), and very low in carbohydrates (3.8 g per 100 g), making them suitable for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI ~15), making this dish appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content is moderate to high due to marinade salting, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension. Prawns are a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and iodine. This dish is popular in North Indian and Mughlai cuisine traditions and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, the United Kingdom, and the Gulf region.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tandoori prawns?
What is zafrani in Indian food?
Is tandoori prawns high in cholesterol?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Zafrani Tandoori Prawns exists in any approved primary source (ICMR-NIN, HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA). Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data with USDA yogurt and cream marinade data. The GI is an analogous estimate based on the protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate composition of the dish. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is transparent, the component sources are approved, and the resulting values are consistent with published ranges for similar tandoori seafood preparations.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN (cooked prawns) and USDA FoodData Central (yogurt, cream) as no exact approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA component sources used for this estimation. 3. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in the component foods per USDA FoodData Central — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for the prawn and dairy components. 4. GI is estimated at 15 based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein composition of the dish; no direct GI measurement for this specific preparation exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 5. Sodium estimate of 420 mg/100g reflects typical restaurant-level salting in tandoori marinades; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant. 6. The dish is served at Zaffron Tandoori, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey listing, price SGD 34.90). 7. Halal status is assumed based on the seafood and dairy composition; kosher status is not confirmed. 8. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component sources and carry medium confidence. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases used.
